content.code:
template<typename T, std::size_t N> // N is a non-type template parameter
struct array;

list: We can ask astd::arrayobject for its length using thesize()member function (which returns the length as unsignedsize_type).In C++17, we can use thestd::size()non-member function (which forstd::arrayjust calls thesize()member function, thus returning the length as unsignedsize_type).In C++20, we can use thestd::ssize()non-member function, which returns the length as a largesignedintegral type (usuallystd::ptrdiff_t).

In C++17, we can use thestd::size()non-member function (which forstd::arrayjust calls thesize()member function, thus returning the length as unsignedsize_type).In C++20, we can use thestd::ssize()non-member function, which returns the length as a largesignedintegral type (usuallystd::ptrdiff_t).
In C++20, we can use thestd::ssize()non-member function, which returns the length as a largesignedintegral type (usuallystd::ptrdiff_t).

Use the subscript operator (operator[]). No bounds checking is done in this case, and passing in an invalid index will result in undefined behavior.Use theat()member function that does subscripting with runtime bounds checking. We recommend avoiding this function since we typically want to do bounds checking before indexing, or we want compile-time bounds checking.Use thestd::get()function template, which takes the index as a non-type template argument, and does compile-time bounds checking.

Use theat()member function that does subscripting with runtime bounds checking. We recommend avoiding this function since we typically want to do bounds checking before indexing, or we want compile-time bounds checking.Use thestd::get()function template, which takes the index as a non-type template argument, and does compile-time bounds checking.
Use thestd::get()function template, which takes the index as a non-type template argument, and does compile-time bounds checking.

Operator=will reseat astd::reference_wrapper(change which object is being referenced).std::reference_wrapper<T>will implicitly convert toT&.Theget()member function can be used to get aT&. This is useful when we want to update the value of the object being referenced.
std::reference_wrapper<T>will implicitly convert toT&.Theget()member function can be used to get aT&. This is useful when we want to update the value of the object being referenced.
Theget()member function can be used to get aT&. This is useful when we want to update the value of the object being referenced.

In C++17, we can use thestd::size()non-member function, which returns the length as unsignedstd::size_t.In C++20, we can use thestd::ssize()non-member function, which returns the length as a largesignedintegral type (usuallystd::ptrdiff_t).
In C++20, we can use thestd::ssize()non-member function, which returns the length as a largesignedintegral type (usuallystd::ptrdiff_t).

content.text: The for-loop has an off-by-one error, and tries to access the array element with index 4, which does not exist.Solution: the conditional in the for loop should use < instead of <=.
Solution: the conditional in the for loop should use < instead of <=.

arraydecays to a pointer when it is passed toprintArray(). Range-based for-loops can’t work with a pointer to an array because the size of the array isn’t known.Solution: usestd::arrayinstead, which doesn’t decay.
Solution: usestd::arrayinstead, which doesn’t decay.
lengthis not a constant expression, and cannot be used to define the length ofstd::array.Solution: use astd::vectorinstead.
Solution: use astd::vectorinstead.

content.code:
// Because identifiers can't start with a number, we'll use a "rank_" prefix for these
enum Rank
{
    rank_ace,
    rank_2,
    rank_3,
    rank_4,
    rank_5,
    rank_6,
    rank_7,
    rank_8,
    rank_9,
    rank_10,
    rank_jack,
    rank_queen,
    rank_king,

    max_ranks
};

// We'll also prefix these for consistency
enum Suit
{
    suit_club,
    suit_diamond,
    suit_heart,
    suit_spade,

    max_suits
};

5H
AC 2C 3C 4C 5C 6C 7C 8C 9C TC JC QC KC AD 2D 3D 4D 5D 6D 7D 8D 9D TD JD QD KD AH 2H 3H 4H 5H 6H 7H 8H 9H TH JH QH KH AS 2S 3S 4S 5S 6S 7S 8S 9S TS JS QS KS

AC 2C 3C
2H 7H 9C

list: The dealer gets one card to start (in real life, the dealer gets two, but one is face down so it doesn’t matter at this point).The player gets two cards to start.The player goes first.A player can repeatedly “hit” or “stand”.If the player “stands”, their turn is over, and their score is calculated based on the cards they have been dealt.If the player “hits”, they get another card and the value of that card is added to their total score.An ace normally counts as a 1 or an 11 (whichever is better for the total score). For simplicity, we’ll count it as an 11 here.If the player goes over a score of 21, they bust and lose immediately.When the player is done, it is the dealer’s turn.The dealer repeatedly draws until they reach a score of 17 or more, at which point they must stop drawing.If the dealer goes over a score of 21, they bust and the player wins immediately.Otherwise, if the player has a higher score than the dealer, the player wins. Otherwise, the player loses (we’ll consider ties as dealer wins for simplicity).

Otherwise, the player loses (we’ll consider ties as dealer wins for simplicity).

content.text: One way would be to keep track of how many aces the player and the dealer got dealt (In thePlayerstruct, as an integer). If either the player or dealer go over 21 and their ace counter is greater than zero, you can reduce their score by 10 (convert an ace from 11 points to 1 point) and decrement the ace counter. This can be done as many times as needed until the ace counter reaches zero.

Our version ofplayBlackjack()currently returns a bool indicating whether the player won or not. We’ll need to update this function to return three possibilities: Dealer win, Player win, tie. The best way to do this would be to define an enumeration for these three options, and have the function return the appropriate enumerator.
